key_points: Population is the total number of people living in a particular area and is an important resource for development
India’s population is unevenly distributed due to variations in climate, terrain, soil fertility and economic opportunities
The Northern Plains and coastal regions are densely populated because of fertile land, good transport and agriculture
Mountainous regions and deserts have sparse population because of harsh climate and poor accessibility
Population density helps measure how crowded a region is and is expressed as persons per square kilometre
Population growth is measured through absolute increase and annual growth rate
Birth rate, death rate and migration are the three main processes influencing population change
Rapid population growth in India occurred due to high birth rates and declining death rates
Migration influences population distribution and urban growth
Adolescents form an important segment of the population and represent the future workforce
examples: Example of dense population: Ganga–Brahmaputra Plains
Example of sparse population: Ladakh region
Example of push factor: Poverty and unemployment in rural areas
Example of pull factor: Job opportunities in cities
Example of urban growth: Expansion of metropolitan cities like Mumbai
Example of adolescent health issue: Iron deficiency leading to anaemia
formulas: Population density = Total population / Total land area
Absolute increase = Population in later year − Population in earlier year
Growth rate = Percentage increase in population over time
Natural increase = Birth rate − Death rate
exam_tips: Understand how physical and economic factors influence population distribution
Remember the difference between population density and population growth
Focus on push and pull factors of migration
Study the importance of adolescent population for development
Learn how census data helps governments plan development policies
